refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss limit). The refined FR isolates the functional requirement—maintaining communication reliability during teleoperation—as a single, atomic obligation in the problem domain. The acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target) is a measurable constraint, not part of the FR itself. This separation allows the FR to remain solution-neutral while the acceptance criterion guides verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profiles, interference scenarios) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold across a statistically significant sample of operational scenarios.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall enable real-time command transmission from tele-operator to remote equipment.
The NGHE solution shall enable real-time telemetry and sensor feedback transmission from remote equipment to tele-operator.
The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication link degradation or loss to the tele-operator and safety system.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time loss detection and network condition feedback
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture with automatic failover to maintain continuity when primary path degrades
DP3: Deploy quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to reserve bandwidth for critical teleoperation commands
DP4: Integrate forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels tuned to measured network conditions
DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission with fallback to reduced-fidelity operation
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Implement a hybrid approach combining loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission with fallback to reduced-fidelity operation

potential_sources: Loss detection mechanism must couple with retransmission logic to avoid redundant recovery attempts
Adaptive buffering strategy must remain decoupled from command prioritization to allow independent tuning
Fallback-to-reduced-fidelity mode must not couple with safety-critical command paths (recommend explicit safety gate)
Network condition feedback loop could couple with QoS if not carefully isolated via clear interfaces
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is selected because it avoids over-commitment to a single recovery strategy (avoiding brittleness) while remaining logically independent from the underlying network transport. The hybrid nature allows graceful degradation and supports the customer-defined acceptance criterion through configurable parameters. Coupling risks are manageable through clear module boundaries and explicit handoff protocols between loss detection, buffering, and retransmission subsystems.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are measurable and stable enough to support adaptive tuning; if conditions are highly volatile, adaptive parameters may oscillate.
ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation commands can be classified into priority tiers (safety-critical vs. non-critical); if all commands are equally critical, fallback-to-reduced-fidelity may not be acceptable.
ASSUMPTION: Acceptable latency budget exists for retransmission cycles; if round-trip time is very short, retransmission overhead may exceed tolerance.
RISK: Adaptive buffering could mask underlying network degradation, delaying operator awareness of unsafe conditions.
RISK: Fallback mode may not be discoverable by operator; recommend explicit UI/alert mechanism to signal mode transition.
RISK: Packet loss acceptance criterion (≤1%) is customer-defined but not yet validated against actual worksite network conditions; recommend pre-deployment network characterization.
RISK: Hybrid approach adds implementation complexity; testing matrix for loss detection + buffering + retransmission + fallback combinations could be large.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication reliability by detecting packet loss in real time, applying adaptive recovery (buffering and selective retransmission), and transitioning to operator-visible reduced-fidelity mode if loss exceeds the customer-defined acceptance criterion (≤1% under agreed network conditions), while preserving safety-critical command integrity through explicit priority gating and operator notification of mode transitions.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote operation logic including network resilience, adaptive quality management, and fallback strategies for maintaining operator control under degraded network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for transmitting control commands and receiving telemetry; loss detection and retransmission logic operates at this boundary.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests vehicle state and sensor data; adaptive buffering and fidelity reduction decisions are informed by telemetry quality and latency metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs adjusted control commands to Vehicle; selective retransmission and reduced-fidelity operation modes alter command structure and frequency.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and communicates fallback-to-reduced-fidelity decisions; ensures safety services are aware of degraded operation mode.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, and reduced-fidelity fallback are all core teleoperation resilience concerns. Network Link and Tasking interfaces naturally bound the DP. Minor diffusion: Safety Services must be notified of mode transitions, but this is a clean outbound alert, not deep coupling.

Overly broad. NGHE Solution is a system-level container. While it could host Teleoperation, placing the DP here obscures ownership and mixes system orchestration with network resilience logic. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation, Vehicle, and Safety Services to all reference NGHE-level policies.

Poor fit. Vehicle is the controlled entity, not the controller. Loss detection and adaptive buffering belong on the operator/teleoperation side. Placing DP here would require Vehicle to manage network resilience for commands it receives, inverting responsibility. High diffusion: Teleoperation would still need to implement complementary logic, creating duplication and inconsistency.

Weak fit. Safety Services should enforce constraints and validate fallback decisions, not implement network resilience mechanisms. Placing DP here conflates safety policy with network adaptation. High diffusion: Teleoperation would still own loss detection and buffering; Safety Services would only see the outcome, not control the mechanism.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of network resilience logic because it sits at the operator-vehicle boundary and controls the fidelity and timing of command transmission. Loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission are all mechanisms for maintaining effective remote control under network stress. Reduced-fidelity fallback is a Teleoperation policy decision that Safety Services validates but does not implement.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large if it also owns command encoding, gesture recognition, and operator feedback rendering. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation focuses narrowly on network resilience and command/telemetry adaptation; delegate UI and encoding to separate modules.

Risk: Reduced-fidelity fallback mode must be coordinated with Safety Services constraints. If safety rules are not consulted before entering reduced-fidelity mode, unsafe operations may occur. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must be bidirectional; Teleoperation queries safety policy before mode transition.

Risk: Adaptive buffering and loss detection require real-time metrics (latency, packet loss rate). If Vehicle does not report these metrics clearly, Teleoperation cannot make informed decisions. Mitigation: Ensure Telemetry interface includes explicit network quality indicators (e.g., round-trip time, loss percentage).

Risk: Selective retransmission logic may conflict with Vehicle's own retry mechanisms if Vehicle also implements loss recovery. Mitigation: Clarify ownership: Teleoperation handles operator-side retransmission; Vehicle handles command-side acknowledgment. Avoid duplication.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The Teleoperation module boundary is well-defined with explicit interfaces (Network Link, Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) that enable measurement of packet loss rates, latency, and fidelity mode transitions. Acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%) is directly observable at the Network Link interface. Telemetry interface must explicitly include network quality indicators (RTT, loss percentage) to support adaptive decision-making.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. Teleoperation owns loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, and reduced-fidelity fallback logic. Vehicle owns command acknowledgment and execution. Safety Services owns constraint validation and fallback authorization. Ownership boundaries are non-overlapping and responsibilities are distinct.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module scope creep. If command encoding, gesture recognition, and operator feedback rendering are co-located with network resilience logic, the module becomes a monolith. Mitigation: Enforce narrow scope—network resilience and command/telemetry adaptation only. Delegate UI and encoding to separate modules.

Risk: Reduced-fidelity fallback mode coordination. If Teleoperation enters reduced-fidelity mode without consulting Safety Services, unsafe operations may occur. Mitigation: SafetyAlert interface must be bidirectional and queried before mode transition, not merely notified after.

Risk: Selective retransmission duplication. If both Teleoperation and Vehicle implement loss recovery, inconsistent behavior and wasted bandwidth may result. Mitigation: Clarify ownership—Teleoperation handles operator-side retransmission; Vehicle handles command-side acknowledgment. Document the boundary explicitly.

Risk: Telemetry interface completeness. Adaptive buffering and loss detection require real-time network quality metrics. If Telemetry interface does not include explicit RTT, packet loss rate, and jitter indicators, Teleoperation cannot make informed decisions. Mitigation: Extend Telemetry interface specification to mandate network quality indicators.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent fit (containment_score 0.92, diffusion_score 0.08). The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because it sits at the operator-vehicle boundary and controls command fidelity and timing. Loss detection, adaptive buffering, and selective retransmission are core teleoperation resilience concerns. The Network Link and Tasking interfaces naturally bound the DP. SafetyAlert is a clean outbound alert interface with no deep coupling. Minor diffusion arises only from the need to notify Safety Services of mode transitions, which is architecturally sound.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid approach combining loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, and fallback to reduced-fidelity operation) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain teleoperation communication reliability within the customer-defined packet loss acceptance criterion (≤ 1%). The DP is logically sound: loss detection identifies degradation, adaptive buffering absorbs transient delays, selective retransmission recovers critical commands, and reduced-fidelity fallback preserves operator control when network conditions are severe. The DP is implementable and measurable against the acceptance criterion.

recommended_revisions: Extend Telemetry interface specification to mandate explicit network quality indicators (round-trip time, packet loss percentage, jitter) so that Teleoperation can make informed adaptive decisions.

Revise SafetyAlert interface to be explicitly bidirectional: Teleoperation must query Safety Services for fallback authorization before entering reduced-fidelity mode, not merely notify after the fact.
Document ownership boundary between Teleoperation (operator-side retransmission) and Vehicle (command-side acknowledgment) to prevent duplication and inconsistency in loss recovery logic.

Define Teleoperation module scope narrowly: network resilience and command/telemetry adaptation only. Delegate command encoding, gesture recognition, and operator feedback rendering to separate modules to prevent scope creep.

Add acceptance criterion verification plan: specify how packet loss rate will be measured at the Network Link interface, how adaptive buffering effectiveness will be validated, and how reduced-fidelity mode transitions will be logged and audited against safety constraints.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR5 and DP5 are logically sound and well-allocated to the Teleoperation module (FR-DP gate: pass, FR-IM gate: pass, DP-IM gate: pass with containment_score 0.92). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that DP5 is a composite strategy with multiple sub-concerns (loss detection, adaptive buffering, selective retransmission, reduced-fidelity fallback, safety coordination) that require explicit decomposition to ensure each concern is independently verifiable, owned, and protected against scope creep. High safety criticality demands that fallback authorization and mode transitions be explicitly modeled as sub-FRs with clear ownership and bidirectional interfaces. Decomposition will also clarify the boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle in loss recovery, and ensure Telemetry interface completeness is captured as a derived requirement. Proceeding to next-level sub-FRs is necessary to operationalize the mitigations and lock in architectural clarity.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Detect network degradation (packet loss, latency, jitter) in real-time and classify severity level (nominal, degraded, critical) to inform adaptive response decisions.
FR5.2: Adapt command and telemetry buffering strategy dynamically based on detected network severity to minimize latency while absorbing transient packet loss without exceeding operator tolerance.

FR5.3: Selectively retransmit critical operator commands (e.g., emergency stop, mode change) on the operator side when loss is detected, with acknowledgment from Vehicle to confirm receipt and execution.

FR5.4: Authorize and execute fallback to reduced-fidelity operation mode (e.g., simplified command set, lower telemetry refresh rate) only when network severity is critical AND Safety Services confirms the fallback mode is safe for the current vehicle state and mission context.

FR5.5: Provide real-time network quality indicators (round-trip time, packet loss percentage, jitter) to Teleoperation via the Telemetry interface to enable informed adaptive decision-making and operator situational awareness.

revise.why: DP5 is a composite strategy that conflates five distinct concerns. Decomposing into sub-FRs (FR5.1–FR5.5) will force explicit DP derivation for each concern, clarifying ownership boundaries (e.g., Teleoperation owns loss detection and operator-side retransmission; Vehicle owns command acknowledgment; Safety Services owns fallback authorization), preventing scope creep, and ensuring each DP is independently verifiable. This also surfaces the bidirectional SafetyAlert interface requirement (FR5.4) and the Telemetry interface completeness requirement (FR5.5) as explicit design constraints rather than post-hoc mitigations.

assumptions: Packet loss, latency, and jitter can be measured reliably at the Network Link interface with sub-millisecond precision.
Operator tolerance for latency is known and can be mapped to buffering strategy thresholds (e.g., buffer up to 200 ms before entering reduced-fidelity mode).
Reduced-fidelity operation modes (simplified command set, lower telemetry rate) are pre-defined and their safety envelopes are validated by Safety Services.
Vehicle can acknowledge receipt and execution of critical commands within a bounded time window (e.g., 500 ms) to enable Teleoperation to detect loss and retransmit.
SafetyAlert interface can support bidirectional queries (Teleoperation → Safety Services: 'Is reduced-fidelity mode X safe in state Y?') with sub-second response time.
open_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface may not include network quality indicators by default. If Vehicle does not expose RTT, packet loss rate, and jitter, Teleoperation cannot make informed adaptive decisions. Mitigation: FR5.5 explicitly requires this; acceptance criterion must verify Telemetry interface includes these fields.

Risk: Reduced-fidelity fallback mode coordination may be asynchronous or delayed. If Teleoperation enters reduced-fidelity mode before Safety Services responds, unsafe operations may occur. Mitigation: FR5.4 requires bidirectional SafetyAlert query with defined timeout and fallback behavior (e.g., if Safety Services does not respond within 100 ms, assume fallback is unsafe and escalate to operator).

Risk: Selective retransmission may conflict with Vehicle-side acknowledgment logic. If both Teleoperation and Vehicle implement loss recovery independently, bandwidth waste and inconsistent behavior may result. Mitigation: FR5.3 and Vehicle-level DP must clarify ownership boundary explicitly (Teleoperation handles operator-side retransmission; Vehicle handles command-side acknowledgment and execution confirmation).

Risk: Operator situational awareness of network degradation may be poor if network quality indicators are not prominently displayed. Mitigation: FR5.5 requires Telemetry interface to include indicators; UI/HMI design (outside Teleoperation module scope) must render these prominently.

Risk: High safety criticality means fallback mode transitions must be auditable and logged. If mode transitions are not explicitly recorded with timestamp, network state, and Safety Services authorization, post-incident analysis may be impossible. Mitigation: FR5.4 acceptance criterion must include logging and audit trail requirements.
evidence_needed: Specification of network quality indicator fields (RTT, packet loss %, jitter) to be included in Telemetry interface; validation that Vehicle can compute and transmit these with acceptable overhead.
Definition of network severity classification thresholds (e.g., nominal: loss < 0.1%, degraded: 0.1% ≤ loss < 1%, critical: loss ≥ 1%) and corresponding buffering/fidelity strategies.
Specification of reduced-fidelity operation modes (command set, telemetry rate, operator feedback) and their safety envelopes (vehicle states, mission contexts in which each mode is safe).
Specification of SafetyAlert interface as bidirectional with query/response semantics, timeout behavior, and fallback logic if Safety Services does not respond within deadline.
Specification of critical command set (e.g., emergency stop, mode change, abort) that requires selective retransmission and Vehicle-side acknowledgment.

Acceptance criterion verification plan: how packet loss rate will be measured at Network Link interface, how adaptive buffering effectiveness will be validated (e.g., latency distribution, operator control fidelity), how reduced-fidelity mode transitions will be logged and audited against safety constraints.
Operator tolerance study or specification: maximum acceptable latency, minimum acceptable telemetry refresh rate, operator feedback on reduced-fidelity mode usability.
